title: Hot and Sour Soup
description: This iconic Chinese Hot and Sour Soup delivers a symphony of balanced flavors – a deep umami broth, a tantalizing kick of white pepper, and a refreshing tang from Chinkiang vinegar. Its delightful medley of textures, from tender shredded pork and silken tofu to chewy mushrooms and crisp bamboo shoots, makes it a beloved comfort dish and a perfect starter for any Chinese meal.

ingredients.notes: Essential for authentic flavor, adjust to taste
Freshly ground is best, adjust to taste for heat

instructions.title: Prepare Ingredients
instructions.text: Rehydrate dried shiitake and wood ear mushrooms in separate bowls of hot water for at least 20 minutes, or until tender. Squeeze out excess water, remove shiitake stems, and thinly slice both types of mushrooms. Shred the firm tofu and bamboo shoots into matchsticks. Marinate the shredded pork with 1 tablespoon light soy sauce and 1 teaspoon cornstarch; set aside. In a small bowl, whisk 3 tablespoons cornstarch with 1/2 cup cold water to create a slurry. Beat the two eggs in another small bowl.

instructions.title: Cook the Pork and Aromatic Base
instructions.text: Heat a wok or large pot over medium-high heat with a tablespoon of cooking oil (not listed as separate ingredient, assuming common pantry item). Add the marinated shredded pork and stir-fry until it turns opaque and is mostly cooked through, about 2-3 minutes. Remove the pork from the pot and set aside.

instructions.title: Simmer the Broth and Vegetables
instructions.text: Pour the 6 cups of chicken or pork broth into the same pot. Bring to a boil, then reduce heat to a simmer. Add the rehydrated shiitake mushrooms, wood ear mushrooms, shredded bamboo shoots, and shredded tofu. Let it simmer gently for 5-7 minutes to allow the flavors to meld. Season the broth with 2 tablespoons light soy sauce and 1/2 teaspoon salt.

instructions.title: Thicken the Soup
instructions.text: Return the cooked shredded pork to the simmering soup. Stir the cornstarch slurry once more to ensure it's well mixed. Slowly pour the slurry into the soup in a thin stream, stirring continuously in one direction (e.g., clockwise) until the soup thickens to your desired consistency. You may not need all of the slurry. The soup should be velvety, not overly thick.

instructions.title: Add the Egg Ribbons
instructions.text: Once the soup is thickened and simmering gently (not boiling rapidly), slowly drizzle the beaten eggs into the soup while stirring continuously with a ladle or spoon. This creates delicate, feathery egg ribbons. Stir gently for about 30 seconds until the egg is cooked.

instructions.title: Finish with Sour and Spice
instructions.text: Remove the pot from the heat. This is crucial for preserving the delicate flavors of the vinegar and pepper. Stir in the 4-5 tablespoons of Chinkiang black vinegar and 1.5-2 teaspoons of white pepper. Taste and adjust the vinegar and white pepper to your preference – it should have a pronounced sour and spicy kick. Drizzle in the 1 teaspoon of sesame oil for aroma. Serve immediately, garnished with thinly sliced scallions.

tips: **Vinegar is Key:** Use authentic Chinkiang (Zhenjiang) black vinegar for the true flavor. Adding it off the heat preserves its volatile aroma and tang.
**White Pepper Power:** Don't skimp on fresh white pepper. It provides the characteristic 'heat' of the soup, distinct from chili heat.
**Gradual Thickening:** Add the cornstarch slurry slowly, stirring constantly, until you reach your desired consistency. You can always add more, but you can't easily remove it.
**Egg Ribbons:** For delicate egg ribbons, ensure the soup is simmering gently (not boiling) when you drizzle in the beaten egg, and stir continuously.
article:
## History of Hot and Sour Soup

Hot and Sour Soup, known as Suan La Tang (酸辣汤) in Mandarin, is a quintessential dish found across China, though its exact origins are often debated between Sichuan and Henan provinces. It's believed to have emerged centuries ago, offering a warming and invigorating experience. The soup's enduring popularity lies in its philosophy of balance – the 'hot' (辣 - là) from white pepper and the 'sour' (酸 - suān) from vinegar, alongside savory umami notes, reflect the Chinese culinary principle of harmonizing flavors. It's not just a dish; it’s a cultural representation of equilibrium, often enjoyed as a comforting staple or a stimulating start to a meal, especially during colder months or to ward off colds.

## Why This Recipe Works

This recipe for Hot and Sour Soup excels because it meticulously balances the five fundamental flavors of Chinese cuisine: sour, sweet, salty, bitter, and umami, with a prominent focus on the 'hot' and 'sour.' The combination of quality chicken or pork broth with rehydrated shiitake and wood ear mushrooms creates a deep, earthy umami base. The precise addition of Chinkiang black vinegar provides a complex, fragrant sourness that is distinct from other vinegars. Freshly ground white pepper delivers a pungent, warming heat that stimulates the palate without overwhelming it. Furthermore, the interplay of textures—silky tofu, chewy mushrooms, crisp bamboo, and tender pork, all suspended in a velvety broth—makes each spoonful a delightful experience, ensuring a satisfying and authentic taste.

## Chef Tips for Perfection

For an exceptional Hot and Sour Soup, prioritize ingredient quality. A rich, homemade chicken or pork broth will elevate the soup's depth significantly. When it comes to the 'sour,' insist on authentic Chinkiang (Zhenjiang) black vinegar; its unique aroma and complex tang are irreplaceable. For the 'hot,' use freshly ground white pepper, as its potency and fragrance are far superior to pre-ground versions. When thickening, always add the cornstarch slurry slowly while stirring, allowing the soup to reach your desired consistency without becoming gloopy. Lastly, add the vinegar and white pepper *after* removing the pot from the heat to preserve their vibrant flavors and aromas, which can dissipate if boiled too long.

## Serving Suggestions

Hot and Sour Soup is incredibly versatile and can be enjoyed in various ways. Traditionally, it serves as a hearty appetizer or a light main course, especially when paired with simple accompaniments. For a classic Chinese meal, serve it alongside steamed rice, pan-fried dumplings (guotie), or crispy spring rolls. It also complements richer main dishes like Kung Pao Chicken or Twice-Cooked Pork, providing a refreshing contrast. For an extra kick, offer a small dish of chili oil on the side for those who prefer more heat. Garnish generously with fresh scallions for a burst of color and a mild oniony crunch, enhancing both its visual appeal and flavor.

jsonLd.name: Hot and Sour Soup
jsonLd.description: This iconic Chinese Hot and Sour Soup delivers a symphony of balanced flavors – a deep umami broth, a tantalizing kick of white pepper, and a refreshing tang from Chinkiang vinegar. Its delightful medley of textures, from tender shredded pork and silken tofu to chewy mushrooms and crisp bamboo shoots, makes it a beloved comfort dish and a perfect starter for any Chinese meal.
jsonLd.keywords: Chinese, Chinese, authentic, traditional, soups, Chinese, authentic, traditional, soups, hot, sour, pork, tofu, mushrooms
recipeIngredient: 6 cups Chicken or Pork Broth (Good quality, low sodium)
6-8 pieces Dried Shiitake Mushrooms (Rehydrated, stems removed, thinly sliced)
1/4 cup Dried Wood Ear Mushrooms (Rehydrated, trimmed, thinly sliced)
1/2 cup Bamboo Shoots (Canned, julienned or shredded)
4 oz (120g) Firm Tofu (Drained, pressed, thinly shredded)
4 oz (120g) Lean Pork (Tenderloin or shoulder, thinly shredded)
1 tbsp Light Soy Sauce (For pork marinade)
2 tbsp Light Soy Sauce (For soup seasoning)
4-5 tbsp Chinkiang Black Vinegar (Essential for authentic flavor, adjust to taste)
1.5 - 2 tsp White Pepper (Freshly ground is best, adjust to taste for heat)

Marinate the shredded pork with 1 tablespoon light soy sauce and 1 teaspoon cornstarch; set aside. In a small bowl, whisk 3 tablespoons cornstarch with 1/2 cup cold water to create a slurry. Beat the two eggs in another small bowl.
